speaker: interviewer
text: This is reel 10, the Interview with Henry Rosmarin. Henry, have you been involved in Holocaust activities over the last 10, 20 years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Even longer than that. When I lived in the Southeast, I spoke-- was rather vocal about speaking about the Holocaust and the Holocaust-related subjects and activities, commemorating Yom HaShoah Days, both in Jewish and non-Jewish communities. Speaking to schools, and some colleges, civic organizations. And in the last-- in California, say, in the last 20 years, I'm a member of various-- in fact, we belong to the congress-- we have, like, three or four societies here. I belong to two of them, to the larger society and also through to the congress of Jewish concentration camp survivors, and another one, Jewish child survivors.

speaker: interviewee
text: So yes, I've been active and also vocal. And I feel almost like I'm duty-bound, on a mission, you know? The incident where my mom pushed me out and said, save yourself. And she said, there will be so much written, talked about. People will never believe what happened, you know. We will survive. We will have a lot to talk about [INAUDIBLE]. I think she-- I think this was the reason-- this was kind of validating.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then again, thank god for-- bless his heart, Steven Spielberg, who made and brought awareness to the mostly non-Jewish community we wanted really to be aware of. And in places where-- I think that was a success all over the world, a success by means of the beautiful story that it told about one human being who could make a difference. And it brought awareness in people who never heard-- generations, second or third generations since war-- since the war-- that didn't know or knew little about the Holocaust. Finally they saw it on the screen and became interested in the story. And so, and that, of course, brought also the creation, thank god, of the Shoah Foundation, of which I'm so fond of. And And my therapist tells me since, he saw a big change in me. I never tire to talk about this mission.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I feel like each survivor wrestles with the same question. Not that anyone found an answer-- at least this one didn't. However, in a certain way, I did. It's that why did I survive, you know? Why was I any better than the next person? The next person was maybe, you know, stronger and maybe more worthy of survival than I am. And better means, and more reasons, you know? And why was I chosen? Why am I so special?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then you carry a certain guilt with it. It comes-- it comes with a certain baggage. But in all fairness, the Shoah Foundation and its work, and the means you've given me, and through you, Steven Spielberg gave us all, and through them, through the camera people and the wonderful people at the Shoah. And to you guys here, gave me a sort of a soapbox. Like, you know, stand up, sit down, and talk, and someone listens. So I'm grateful for that opportunity. But also, speaking out and volunteering and being-- or sometimes doing some liaison work with talking with other fellow survivors, I feel-- I feel like we are a close-knit family. And as I told you, my therapist calls it the [? family ?] that maybe-- he says, Henry, you know, maybe this Shoah Project validates some of your ancestors and validates the reason you survived. And so that in itself is a tremendous engine that powers this energy into speaking and being vocal. And as long as the one above gives me the opportunity, gives me the soundness of mind and the ability of expressing, I will be speaking, or singing, or playing about it. So, yes. That's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any message that you would like to send--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh gosh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The message to put-- it's very hard to put everything I would like to say in a message all in a capsule form. [SIGHS] But it all boils down to the basic understanding and-- and-- that, A, I think tolerance is probably the biggest ingredient in my message. That unless we are tolerant, unless we learn to love, respect, tolerate someone's religion, tolerate another language, be understanding to his or her accent or his or her looks, or the color of-- the outward color of ones-- a human being, and how much-- and if we don't do it, then look-- look what can happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much more proof does the free world-- how much more proof do the naysayers, the agitators, the hate mongers-- how much more proof do they need other than to look at what hate can do? What kind of a boiling point? How much hate you can propagandize? With and-- What will be the final outcome? If-- if you keep preaching hate, pretty soon you'll have a beast like the SS, like the Nazis, who were throwing babies against a blank wall or throwing them into box cars like-- like-- like rag dolls. What kind of a-- kind of a human being-- we are all made in the image of the one above, and how-- what kind of a human being does it take to do this, this kind of horrible act? Can a human being be poisoned with hate so much that-- that they don't-- they are blind to these kind of murderous acts? So our generation-- it is the survivors, should be-- should be the-- the examples saying to the world, look, world, the [INAUDIBLE] and the Tutsis, pay attention. The Bosnians and the Serbs and-- and-- and the Muslims and the Jews, and who-- this is what's going to happen if we don't-- if we don't-- if we don't learn to live with one another in peace.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this lesson, as-- as bitter as it was, let this be a lesson in that it isn't-- because if it hasn't-- didn't serve as a lesson, then-- then my poor parents and [INAUDIBLE] the millions of-- of innocent people would have died in vain. And they are speaking out in-- in today's-- thank god, in today's high-tech world where we can preserve these testimonies, maybe-- maybe this message will linger for a generation-- two, five, or maybe 10, we would like to hope. And-- and the future generations would have learned a lesson, and they say, you know, it was a generation before us. This is what would happen if-- if we don't-- if we don't make peace accords. And if we-- and if we-- This is what can happen. We have to be tolerant. We have to live together in peace. We all-- we have a limited time here. We all are God's children. And there's no use to blame one God or other gods or my god or your God. The one above didn't create this. All of these horrible stories we talked about, and that's been talked about on thousands, perhaps, of these interviews, were manmade. God didn't make them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my message, again-- please, please learn from our example. And let us-- let us live in peace. Respect the few-- help fellow human beings. Everyone was created equal. Each of us has a right to live, and let him live in peace. And I want to end this with that message. Thank you, Henry.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to play us a piece, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. You know-- this time I'd like to play a little harmonica. This-- it's kind a-- It's a sensitive, little instrument. And I would like to do some liturgy. I'll have a drink of water. I'm kind of dry. I've been talking too much. [DRINKS] With a little medley from our-- from our liturgy from the synagogue. One is [? a little more ?] [INAUDIBLE], sometime before. I'll do a little bit of it, and the other one, which will end-- which will fit to my final message, [HEBREW], peace unto to you, I believe. Yes. [PLAYING HARMONICA]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Henry, would you introduce us to this lovely lady?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is Jadzia Yakubowicz of the ghetto, and formally Yakubowicz. Now It's my wife, Janet Rosmarin. My one and only love.

speaker: interviewer
text: Janet, would you like to say a few words?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm glad he feels this way about me because I feel the same about him. As Henry said, we went through a lot. And it sure was a miracle that we met again. And thank god we're here.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you reunited in Czeladz--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: --53 years ago?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think you told the story how we met?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was at services.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, services. And then after the war, I didn't believe it that we meet again. But that was fate, and we're here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell a little bit about-- just very briefly talk about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I also was in Auschwitz. And this is the number. And that's why it's a miracle that we're here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And still together. Thank you both very, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: As you can see, my wife is a lady of a few words.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after so many hours which you've been talking, so I don't have much to say. And actually, well, I have that we're very happy, and it was a love from first sight. And life goes on, thank god.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you both very, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. What we're looking at now is pictures-- the only pictures I have from before the war. The first one on the left, standing, going from left to right-- my mother, Helen Rosmarin, her sister-- they look almost like twins, her sister-- Sally Schwartzfelt, a neighbor lady, and a niece of my mother's to the right. Her name is Sophie. Sitting on the left, from left to right again, that's the only picture of me as a young boy. I was here, perhaps, 12. And this little kid in the center is [INAUDIBLE], my cousin. And also my cousin, Sally's daughter, and my cousin, Regina, who later found Jadzia for me. The year was 1938. 1938 in Lelow, Poland. What we are seeing now is a picture of myself and my brother. I am on the left with the harmonica. To the right is my brother, Max. The year is 1940, the first fall-- the first year in the ghetto. I'm about 14, 14 and 1/2 here, and my brother is 16. The year is 1940, and it's taken at Czeladz, Poland. This is a postcard written in the ghetto, in Czeladz Ghetto-- the last piece of mail getting out of the ghetto. The date is April, 1943. This was before judenrat, cleansing of Jews. And the postcard is addressed to Esther Rinska, who later became my sister-in-law, the wife of my late brother. She also passed away. And in it, the parents are writing sort of a goodbye letter, a message that they're talking about deportations. And they were saying that they might be reunited with Uncle [INAUDIBLE]. Uncle [INAUDIBLE] passed away before the war. This is a way for them saying that they are expecting to be deported to who knows where. And the letter Z in the center means it's been censored, gone through censorship. 'Censorship' in German is spelled with a Z. And the guy on the postage stamp-- I don't need to tell you who that is. That's [INAUDIBLE]. And again, the date is April 1943. The last piece of mail-- the last living written piece of mail from out of the ghetto. This picture is of friends of Jadzia, including mine. I was taking the picture. I'm in the picture. The picture was taken in the Czeladz ghetto. The year, 1940. And two survived after the war, Jadzia and [INAUDIBLE], the two girls in the center. This is Jadzia. This is what she looked like when I fell in love with her. Czeladz, 1940. This is an ID document. This is the one the Russian officer initially gave me right after the war. It's written in four languages-- French, German, Russian, and English. And gives the identity of me, and who I was and what camp and all my personal identity. This was like travel documents to get me through to Poland and through various borders. The date is 1945-- summer of 1945. This is a commemoration of what is now known as Yom HaShoah Day in May. And then we commemorated first liberation date, May of 1946, on the grounds of a concentration camp, Ebensee, in Upper Austria. This is May of 1946. The picture-- I am holding the wreath in the center. To my left is then my girlfriend, Jadzia, my present wife, Janet Rosmarin. This is our wedding picture, the original wedding picture. The date was November 23, 1947 in Bad Ischl, also Upper Austria, within 20 minutes drive of the camp, which I spoke about earlier, Ebensee. The picture was taken by a photographer, [INAUDIBLE]. And the reason I mention his name because later on we had our picture taken also by him. This is November 23, 1947. My wife, Janet, and I. And this is a picture, again, of this handsome couple, I must say. We went back to Austria, would you believe, to the same photographer, [INAUDIBLE]. His son was running the business, but he came. I called him ahead of time. He was still alive. And he says, yeah, he'd be happy to receive us. And he came down to the studio and personally took our picture. We reminisced about 50 years ago in November of 1947. This was done last year, 1997, also in Bad Ischl, Austria. And as I said, taken by the same photographer. They were on the local television. They ran on a story about us, also interviewed us with the photographer as a human interest story over half a century. This is a picture of myself. It actually says [? Henry Rowin ?]. That was my stage name then. I worked professionally for a few years when I was younger-- I must say much younger. And I traveled extensively for a lot of USO camp shows, military installation. For one, I felt like I should pay back some of what they did for us. And I traveled and played my harmonica and worked professionally in supper clubs in those days. We're talking late '50s, early '60s. And it's a composite of one person, of myself. I played at various stages with various harmonicas. And that's the story. This is a photo of my son, Marvin. It was taken at our golden wedding anniversary. This is May at the [INAUDIBLE] Center. And we had a wonderful time. And our son Marvin got dressed up for the occasion. This is our younger son, Harry, Harry Benjamin. He's sitting in a Vegas restaurant having a good time, eating a delicious meal. The year-- I believe this was 1996.
